retrieved_docs.content: According to WHO’s Global Burden of Disease Project 2000 (GBD 2000), iron deficiency is responsible for 841,000 deaths worldwide with the major burden of mortality seen in Africa and parts of Asia. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Disagreement also exists among the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF), various medical academic institutions, and actual clinical practice regarding anemia screening guidelines. For example, the USPSTF evaluated anemia screening in asymptomatic children aged 6 to 24 months and concluded that evidence was insufficient to determine the benefits versus harms of screening in this population. In contrast, the American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP) recommended universal anemia screening at 12 months of age, including hemoglobin measurement and an assessment of risk factors for iron deficiency and iron deficiency anemia (IDA). [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Deficiency Anemia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Although iron deficiency is one of the oldest and most common medical disorders, the condition still has not received adequate clinical attention and evaluation. Many children, elderly patients, and pregnant women continue to have undiagnosed iron deficiency anemia or remain under-treated. Evidence from an interprofessional panel of clinicians reveals that iron-deficiency anemia has a high prevalence in hospitalized patients. It is associated with worse outcomes, including more extended hospital stays and poor quality of life. There are also risks for those who receive blood transfusions. The panel has recommended several strategies in early diagnosis, treatment, and follow up of these patients. [11] The most critical recommendation is a prompt referral to a specialist; not all causes of iron deficiency anemia are merely due to a gastrointestinal bleed or heavy menstrual cycles. The primary care health care provider plays a vital role as he or she is almost always the first to note the presence of iron deficiency anemia. Others who are essential in detecting iron-deficiency anemia include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Lead levels: This test assesses lead toxicity, which can contribute to anemia through disrupted hemoglobin synthesis, particularly in children.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Iron Deficiency -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic iron deficiency is a global health problem. It is easy to overlook it as the presentation is often vague and nonspecific. In developing countries, inadequate dietary intake is the leading cause of chronic iron deficiency, whereas hemorrhage is the leading cause of chronic iron deficiency in developed nations. The causes of iron deficiency could be due to a variety of gynecological, obstetrical, metabolic, and gastrointestinal etiologies. The physical exam is often normal, and the cause cannot be determined without lab investigations.

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Epidemiological reporting of anemia is fragmented due to varying diagnostic criteria, with the U.S. using different standards than the WHO criteria, which are applied globally. Additionally, anemia prevalence varies across demographic, genetic, and geographic subsets. Best estimates suggest similar anemia prevalence in the U.S., Canada, and northern Europe, with approximately 4% of male individuals and 8% of female individuals meeting diagnostic thresholds in these regions. Data for the rest of the world remain more limited. Although available estimates are inexact, findings indicate that anemia occurs at rates 2 to 5 times higher worldwide than in the U.S., Canada, and northern Europe. [14] [15] [16] Regions wherein populations commonly show higher rates of anemia include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Although the journal acknowledged significant differences in hemoglobin levels among Black men and women, it did not establish diagnostic thresholds for these populations. Most current literature continues to use WHO standards for consistency.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Deficiency Anemia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 25% of people worldwide have anemia. Iron deficiency, the most common cause, is responsible for 50% of all anemias. The rate of iron deficiency is higher in developing countries compared to the United States, where the prevalence of iron-deficiency anemia in men under 50 is 1%. In women of childbearing age in the United States, the rate is 10% due to losses from menstruation, while 9% of children ages 12 to 36 months are iron-deficient, and one-third of these children develop anemia. While the rate of iron-deficiency anemia is low in the United States, low-income families are particularly at risk. [7] [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Iron Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: TSAT is calculated as the ratio of serum iron to TIBC. It is one of the earliest biomarkers of iron deficiency and is useful when serum ferritin is unequivocal. A decrease (less than 20%) indicates an iron deficiency, either absolute or functional. [15] [3] Hemoglobin does not become low until a significant percentage of body iron becomes depleted. Hence, normal hemoglobin does not exclude iron deficiency. Other iron studies available for the evaluation of iron deficiency are:

retrieved_docs.title: Iron-Binding Capacity -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia is the most common hematological disorder in all age groups, and iron-deficiency anemia is the most commonly encountered anemia worldwide. [8] [9] Approximately 30% of the global population is affected by iron-deficiency anemia. [10] Iron studies are routinely performed in patients with anemia, particularly if the anemia is normocytic or microcytic.

By delivering iron together with folic acid and a broad spectrum of vitamins, minerals, fatty acids, and supportive agents, IRON/MULTIVITAMINS aim to correct iron‑deficiency anemia, prevent or treat folate‑deficiency‑related macrocytic anemia, and address overall nutritional needs, thereby providing a comprehensive micronutrient therapy for patients at risk of hematologic and nutritional disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1444
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as an “Other deficiency anemia.” The category of Other deficiency anemias includes folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), pernicious anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease belongs to this broader group highlights shared pathogenic mechanisms among these deficiency‑related anemias and guides clinicians toward comprehensive evaluation and management strategies that address the underlying nutrient deficiencies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2281
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as an Other deficiency anemia. The category Other deficiency anemias includes فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia), and فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (non‑immune hemolytic anemia). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease falls within this broader group of deficiency‑related anemias helps clinicians consider overlapping etiologies, appropriate diagnostic work‑up, and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل), threatened miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد). These associations highlight the importance of evaluating and managing metabolic, hematologic, and early‑pregnancy complications when counseling patients about elective pregnancy termination, ensuring comprehensive risk assessment and optimal maternal health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1384
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as a form of “Other deficiency anemias.” The category of Other deficiency anemias encompasses several distinct types, including فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية (autoimmune hemolytic anemia), and فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (non‑immune hemolytic anemia). Recognizing that anemia associated with chronic kidney disease falls within this broader group helps clinicians consider the underlying nutritional or hemolytic mechanisms that may contribute to the patient’s low hemoglobin and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and targeted treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 954
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as belonging to the broader category of Other deficiency anemias. The category Other deficiency anemias encompasses several specific deficiency disorders, including فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم الوبيل (a form of vitamin‑B12‑related anemia), and فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia). In addition, Other deficiency anemias has a child condition called فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (non‑megaloblastic anemia). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease falls within this group of deficiency‑related anemias guides clinicians to assess for underlying nutrient deficiencies and related subtypes when diagnosing and treating affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1489
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia (فقر الدم) is encompassed within the broader category of deficiency and other anemia, indicating that it can arise from nutritional deficiencies as well as other etiologies. Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific subtype that falls under the parent condition of anemia, representing a rapid loss of blood leading to reduced hemoglobin levels. Clinically, anemia is closely associated with the symptom of weakness (الشعور بالضعف), a common manifestation that aids in the recognition and management of the disorder. Understanding that anemia, whether due to deficiency, acute hemorrhage, or other causes, frequently presents with weakness is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 821
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as an other deficiency anemia. The category of other deficiency anemias includes the conditions فقر الدم الوبيل and فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات, and it also has a subcategory called فقر الدم اللا تنسجي. This classification links anemia in chronic kidney disease to a broader group of deficiency‑related anemias and identifies specific related subtypes within that group.

retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplements; they contain both vitamin B12 and folic acid and are used to treat or prevent anemia that results from deficiencies of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is recognized as an etiological descriptor for several medical conditions, including iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد), transfusion reaction (تفاعل نقل الدم), mortality (التموت), and acute yellow liver congestion (ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ). By serving as an allowed etiological qualifier, it helps clinicians attribute these conditions to specific underlying causes, thereby supporting accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient management.

Clinically, folic acid is directly linked to the prevention and treatment of macrocytic anemia (فقر دم ضَخم الأرومات), a condition that arises from folate deficiency and is characterized by the production of abnormally large red blood cells. By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, these products aim to correct or prevent folate‑related hematologic abnormalities while simultaneously providing broader micronutrient support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 711
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified among other deficiency anemias. The category of other deficiency anemias includes folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), pernicious anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). Additionally, other deficiency anemias are related to cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease belongs to this broader group helps clinicians consider overlapping pathophysiological mechanisms and select appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

By providing folic acid together with iron and other B‑vitamins, these multivitamin‑iron supplements aim to prevent or correct folate deficiency‑related macrocytic anemia, support hematopoiesis, and address the increased nutritional needs of patients such as those undergoing bariatric surgery, individuals with dietary insufficiencies, or pregnant women.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1538
retrieved_docs.content: Other deficiency anemias is a broad diagnostic category that includes several specific types of anemia. It is classified as فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia) and as فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia), reflecting its inclusion of nutrient‑deficiency–related blood disorders. In addition, the category is classified as فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (non‑immune‑mediated hemolytic anemia), indicating that it also encompasses hemolytic anemias that are not caused by autoimmunity. Within this grouping, فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia) is identified as a child or subcategory, further specifying a type of hemolysis that develops secondary to external factors. Together, these relationships show that “Other deficiency anemias” serves as an umbrella term that integrates both deficiency‑based anemias (folate and iron) and certain non‑immune hemolytic anemias, including acquired forms, which is important for accurate diagnosis, classification, and patient management.
